next_steps: Reverse demo scheduled for Thursday to review quoting process and calculations
Prospect's boss (Head of Commercial Operations), VP of Sales, and CEO in buying committee
buying_committee: Head of Commercial Operations (prospect's boss, needs demo/trial)

jtbd.job: Enforce pricing consistency and discount governance across 200 quoting users without manual review
jtbd.context: Sales reps can download templates and edit them freely. No controls on discounts or special terms. Managers co-sign quotes but there is no systematic policy enforcement.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Automated approval workflows triggered by discount thresholds so pricing stays within policy without every quote requiring manual oversight

jtbd.job: Support three distinct pricing methods (square-meter, project-cost, annual-turnover) within a single quoting tool
jtbd.context: Nine products with three pricing methods: square-meter-based, construction-cost-based (multi-currency), and annual-turnover-based (per-mill calculation). HubSpot native line items are quantity-based only and cannot model these.
jtbd.desired_outcome: A quoting tool that handles formula-based pricing with variable inputs (area, project cost, currency, turnover) and calculates prices automatically

jtbd.job: Centralize quoting inside HubSpot after CRM migration
jtbd.context: Recently migrated from Pipedrive to HubSpot. Currently quoting through DocuSign which is disconnected from the CRM. Want a native HubSpot solution.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Single quoting workflow inside HubSpot that captures pricing, discounts, and terms in the CRM for reporting and governance

pains.pain: HubSpot native CPQ cannot model their pricing: quantity-based tiers only, no formula-based pricing, percentage-only discounts
pains.trigger: Attempted to configure HubSpot products and line items for their three pricing methods. Built workflow workarounds but hit limitations on tier-based pricing and discount types (no flat-amount discounts).
pains.cost: Cannot use HubSpot for quoting at all. Products are not in HubSpot product library. All pricing lives outside the CRM.
pains.urgency_reason: This is the core blocker preventing them from moving quoting into HubSpot

pains.pain: No discount visibility or governance across 200 quoting users
pains.trigger: Reps can download templates and edit them. No way to track how many discounts are being given or what special terms and conditions are being added.
pains.cost: Revenue operations cannot report on discounting patterns. Unknown margin impact across 200 users.
pains.urgency_reason: Prospect named pricing and approvals as top two priorities for the evaluation

pains.pain: Template control is impossible when reps can download and edit freely
pains.trigger: Templates exist but are not enforced. Reps download, modify content, terms, and pricing, then send via DocuSign.
pains.cost: Inconsistent customer-facing documents. No audit trail of what was actually sent. RevOps cannot ensure brand or policy compliance.
pains.urgency_reason: Prospect described wanting flexibility for reps while maintaining control, indicating this is a recognized tension but not the top priority

pains.pain: Multi-currency pricing complexity across global operations
pains.trigger: Construction-cost-based pricing requires converting project costs from local currency (USD, EUR, DKK, etc.) into product pricing. Manual calculation across 25 offices.
pains.cost: Pricing errors from manual currency-based calculations. Complexity multiplied across global team.
pains.urgency_reason: Mentioned as part of pricing complexity but not flagged as a standalone burning issue

pains.pain: DocuSign is a signing tool, not a quoting tool, but it is being used for quoting
pains.trigger: DocuSign works for collecting signatures but cannot handle price calculations, approval workflows, or reporting on quote content.
pains.cost: No CPQ capabilities: no price books, no calculated fields, no approval routing, no discount controls. Purely a document signature tool being stretched beyond its purpose.
pains.urgency_reason: DocuSign renewal in August-September 2026 creates a natural decision window

workflow_reality.current_process: Reps build quotes manually using downloadable templates, calculate pricing outside of HubSpot (likely spreadsheets given the three pricing methods), and send completed documents through DocuSign for signature. Manager co-signature required on every quote. No pricing data flows back into HubSpot.
tools_in_use: HubSpot CRM (migrated from Pipedrive in 2025, used for pipeline, not quoting)
Manual pricing calculations (three methods: square-meter, project-cost, annual-turnover)
brittleness_indicators: Products are not in HubSpot product library — pricing lives entirely outside the CRM
Reps can download and edit templates without restrictions
No way to track discounts or special terms being offered
Manager co-signature is the only control mechanism — no automated policy enforcement
Prospect built HubSpot workflow workarounds for pricing but hit tier and discount-type limitations
Construction industry context: DocuSign was a recent adoption; prior process was signing by hand

evaluated.name: DealHub
evaluated.why_they_passed: Requires too much admin overhead. Prospect is the sole admin and DealHub demands more resources than available.
evaluated.key_quote: We looked at DealHub, but it's totally me as an admin. And that requires much more.

evaluated.name: HubSpot Commerce Hub
evaluated.why_they_passed: Still in evaluation. Prospect already identified HubSpot's line item and pricing limitations as blockers.
evaluated.key_quote: Their line items and products are all quantity based, and the tiers that you set are also based by quantity.

evaluated.name: Contractbook
evaluated.why_they_passed: Still in evaluation. No further detail provided.

current_workaround.description: Downloadable templates sent through DocuSign with manual pricing calculations and manager co-signature as the only governance mechanism
current_workaround.adequacy: Functional for collecting signatures but provides zero pricing governance, no discount tracking, and no reporting. Prospect explicitly stated they need to change if they want anything more advanced.

quotes.quote: For quoting, we are currently using DocuSign. We were looking into changing things mainly due because of CPQ and getting more sense of our pricing as well as approvals and getting some more cohesive version.
quotes.usage_notes: Clean articulation of the two core needs: pricing intelligence and approval workflows. Good for RevOps persona content about outgrowing basic signing tools.

quotes.quote: We have some templates now, but people can still download them and edit them. It makes it more difficult for me to find out how many discounts are we getting and any of this kind of stuff. What are all the special terms and conditions?
quotes.usage_notes: Captures the RevOps governance pain perfectly. Useful for content about template control and discount visibility. The question format makes it especially relatable.

quotes.quote: Their line items and products are all quantity based, and the tiers that you set are also based by quantity. They can only give percentage discount. They cannot give direct, like, euros or dollars discounts.
quotes.usage_notes: Specific, technical articulation of HubSpot CPQ limitations from someone who tried to make it work. Strong for comparison content and HubSpot CPQ limitation articles.

quotes.quote: I figured out a way with workflows and stuff on how to do pricing, but then it always ends up being on a tier anyway.
quotes.usage_notes: Shows the RevOps persona trying to make HubSpot work and hitting the wall. Validates the 'outgrown HubSpot CPQ' narrative. Good for content targeting RevOps who are building workarounds.

quotes.quote: We looked at DealHub, but it's totally me as an admin. And that requires much more.
quotes.usage_notes: Direct competitive intelligence. DealHub lost on admin overhead for a team-of-one RevOps. Strong for DealHub comparison content and positioning Quotivity's ease of administration.

quotes.quote: DocuSign is working fine as a signing tool, just for collecting signatures. But if we want to do something more advanced, I think we need to change.
quotes.usage_notes: Clear articulation of the status quo ceiling. Useful for content about when signing tools stop being enough.

quotes.quote: We would like to have something native within HubSpot, that integrates well with HubSpot.
quotes.usage_notes: Validates the HubSpot-native positioning directly. Useful across all persona content where native integration is the differentiator.

quotes.quote: It was not recently that we got DocuSign. It was signed by hand before.
quotes.usage_notes: Reveals construction industry context: digital adoption is recent. Useful for industry-specific content about construction SaaS companies modernizing their sales process.

value_language: getting more sense of our pricing
choose the right platform for the future

deviant_terms.meaning: The ability to send any document for signature outside of the template/quoting workflow. Used as an internal term for ad-hoc document signing (e.g., customer-originated order forms that only need internal signature).
deviant_terms.frequency: used three times, clearly an established internal term

deviant_terms.meaning: Pricing calculated as a per-mille (per thousand) rate of the customer's annual turnover. Industry-specific pricing method.
deviant_terms.frequency: used once but describes a core pricing method

deviant_terms.meaning: Used to describe urgency level. Prospect explicitly said 'it's not a burning platform' meaning no crisis forcing immediate action.

keyword_candidates.source: direct pain — prospect named approvals as a top-two priority and has no automated approval process today

keyword_candidates.source: direct pain — cannot track discounts, no flat-amount discounts possible, no visibility into what reps are offering

keyword_candidates.source: prospect evaluated DealHub and passed due to admin overhead — direct competitive displacement signal

keyword_candidates.source: prospect has three pricing methods (area-based, cost-based, turnover-based) that HubSpot cannot model
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Formula-based pricing in HubSpot: how to quote beyond simple quantity x unit price

keyword_candidates.source: prospect needs multiple price books with override capability — HubSpot does not support multiple price books natively

keyword_candidates.source: prospect sells SaaS to construction industry with industry-specific pricing (square meters, project construction costs)
